items.title: Day 76: Python Binary Exponentiation – O(log n) Fast Power Calculation Without Built-Ins
items.author: Shahrouz Nikseresht
items.content:
Welcome to Day 76 of the journey! This intermediate challenge implements binary exponentiation to compute x^n in O(log n) time, using bit manipulation and iterative squaring to avoid slow naive multiplication loops or recursion. It handles positive/negative exponents and floats, making it a versatile math tool for algorithms, cryptography, or simulations. If you're optimizing power calculations or prepping for interviews with fast expo questions (LeetCode #50 style), this "Python binary exponentiation" script demonstrates a function that's efficient, handles edges, and easy to adapt for modular expo in crypto.

💡 Key Takeaways from Day 76: Fast Power Function

This task features a function that reduces exponent by half each step, squaring base and multiplying on odd bits. It's a divide-and-conquer pattern: halve exponent, square base. We'll detail: function with negative handling and result init, loop for bit-based multiply and square, and .

1. Function Design: Negative Expo and Base Cases

The function takes base x and exponent n, returns float:

Handles n=0 as 1. For negative, invert x and make n positive. Result starts 1 for multiply accumulation.

2. Loop Processing: Square and Multiply on Bits

If n odd (LSB 1), multiply result by x. Always square x, halve n. For x=2 n=5 (101b): result*2 (bit1), square to 4, n=2; square to 16, n=1; result*16 (bit1), n=0 → 32. O(log n) steps.

3. Main Interactive: Float Base and Int Expo Input

Script prompts x/n, computes:

Float base for decimals, int expo. Simple, assumes valid.

🎯 Summary and Reflections

This binary expo halves work for fast powers. It reinforced:

  • : n%2 for LSB.
  • : x*=x halves problem.
  • : Simple reciprocal.

Reflections: Modular version (x^n mod m) key for crypto (RSA). Handles large n without overflow via Python bigints.

: Recursive: if n even (x^{n/2})^2 else x*(x^{n-1}). Matrix for Fibonacci. Your expo trick? Share!

items.title: Maximize efficiency with professional Odoo customization services

A seasoned Odoo development company optimizes Odoo’s performance by refining database queries, adjusting server resources, restructuring modules, and improving front-end load times. This produces a stable, fast system, even as operations multiply across regions.

Industry-specific customizations that deliver real results

Every sector in the Middle East has its own operational patterns. Customization aligns Odoo with these patterns precisely.

Construction & real estate

Developers configure role-based security rules, field-level restrictions, and workflow checkpoints to maintain regulatory adherence across departments.

Scalable systems that grow with your business

Enterprises in the Middle East expand rapidly across new regions and verticals. Odoo’s modular structure supports this growth, but customization ensures the scaling process remains smooth. Customized setups enable:

How to choose the right Odoo customization partner

items.title: Scrapy Logging: The Complete Beginner's Guide (Debug Like a Pro)
items.author: Muhammad Ikramullah Khan
items.content:
When I first started using Scrapy, my spiders would just... stop working. No error messages. No clues. Just silence.

I'd stare at my terminal thinking "Did it even run? Where did it fail? Why isn't anything happening?"

Then I learned about logging. Suddenly, my spiders started talking to me. They'd tell me exactly what they were doing, where they were going, what they found, and when things went wrong.

Logging transformed me from a confused beginner to someone who could actually debug problems. Let me show you how to make your spiders communicate clearly.

Think of logging like a diary your spider keeps. As it runs, it writes down everything it does:

  • "Uh oh, I got a 404 error"
  • "I'm done, here are my stats"

Without logging, your spider runs in complete silence. You have no idea what's happening inside.

With logging, you see everything. Every step. Every decision. Every problem.

The Five Log Levels (From Loud to Quiet)

Python has five log levels. Think of them like volume settings:

Everything. Every tiny detail. Use this when you're hunting bugs.

Important milestones. "I started," "I found data," "I finished."

Something weird happened, but the spider keeps running.

Something broke, but the spider continues with other pages.

Everything is on fire. The spider can't continue.

By default, Scrapy shows INFO and above. DEBUG messages are hidden unless you ask for them.

Every spider has a built-in logger. Just use :

You'll see your log messages mixed with Scrapy's own messages.

Change Log Level from Command Line

Only show warnings and errors:

scrapy crawl myspider WARNING

Show everything, including debug messages:

scrapy crawl myspider DEBUG

Change Log Level in Settings

Console output disappears when you close the terminal. Save logs to a file instead:

scrapy crawl myspider spider.log

Now all your logs save to . Perfect for production scrapers that run for hours.

Append to Existing Log File

By default, Scrapy overwrites the log file each time. To keep old logs and append new ones:

Real-World Example: A Logging Spider

Let's build a spider that logs everything important:

scrapy crawl products INFO products.log

Your log file will have a complete record of what happened.

Advanced: Custom Log Formatting

Scrapy's default logs look like this:

2024-12-24 10:30:15 [myspider] INFO: Scraped product: Widget

You can customize the format:

INFO: Scraped product: Widget

2024-12-24 10:30:15 [INFO] Scraped product: Widget

2024-12-24 10:30:15 [myspider] INFO: Scraped product: Widget

Pipelines don't have like spiders do. Create your own:

Scrapy logs A LOT. Sometimes too much. Here's how to quiet specific parts:

Hide Specific Log Categories

When scraping, you'll often hit 404s or 500s. These create lots of WARNING logs. To hide them:

Show Only Your Spider's Logs

Now you'll only see your spider's log messages.

With , you get the full error traceback in your logs. Super helpful for debugging.

Tip #1: Log Data Quality Issues

This helps you spot issues with the website's data, not just your selectors.

Tip #2: Log Progress for Long Scrapes

For spiders that run for hours, this shows you're still making progress.

Tip #3: Log What You're NOT Scraping

Knowing what you skipped helps debug incomplete data.

Tip #4: Different Logs for Different Spiders

Running multiple spiders? Keep their logs separate:

scrapy crawl spider1 spider1.log
scrapy crawl spider2 spider2.log

Mistake #1: Using print() Instead of Logging

doesn't respect log levels or file output. Always use the logger.

Mistake #2: Logging Too Much in Production

In production with thousands of items, logging each one creates massive log files.

Mistake #3: Not Logging Failures

Production Spider with Complete Logging

Here's a complete, production-ready spider with proper logging:

  • All errors with full details

scrapy crawl myspider DEBUG


scrapy crawl myspider spider.log


scrapy crawl myspider INFO spider.log

Logging is your spider's voice. It tells you:

  • What it's doing right now
  • Five levels: DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL
  • Save logs to files for production
  • Log errors with for stack traces
  • Log progress for long-running scrapers
  • Use appropriate log levels (don't log everything as INFO!)

Start adding logs to your spiders today. When something breaks (and it will), you'll have the information you need to fix it quickly.

items.title: Doc-First Development: Because 'We'll Document It Later' Is a Lie
items.author: Mr. 0x1
items.content:
After 20+ years in software, I've seen every approach to building systems. This is the one that actually works.

Every time I mention "doc-first" or "spec-driven development" to engineers, I get blank stares.

"That sounds great in a perfect world."

Here's the thing: We're software engineers. We create worlds. Why wouldn't we try to make them perfect?

This article—and the complete codebase on GitHub—exists to demonstrate that spec-first isn't some ivory tower ideal. It's practical. It's powerful. And with the tools we have today, it's easier than ever.

is a complete event-driven order processing system built entirely spec-first:

  1. Specifications are the source of truth — OpenAPI 3.1 for REST, AsyncAPI 3.0 for events
  2. Code is generated from specs — Types, interfaces, clients, event handlers
  3. Conformance tests validate everything — Implementation match the specification
  4. Real infrastructure via Testcontainers — NATS, PostgreSQL, Redis spin up for tests

The result? A system where the contract is king, drift is impossible, and tests prove conformance—not just behavior.

📐 The Doc-First Philosophy

Traditional development flows like this:

Write code → Document later (maybe) → Hope nothing drifts

Doc-first development flips the script:

Write spec → Generate code → Implement interfaces → Prove conformance

When code is generated from specs:

  • ✅ between documentation and implementation
  • ✅ guaranteed by the spec
  • ✅ for any language
  • ✅ , never backwards

Conformance Over Coverage

Traditional testing asks: "Does the code do what the code says?"

Conformance testing asks: "Does the code do what the **contract* says?"*

One tests implementation. The other tests .

Orders flow through three Watermill-powered stages:

Check required fields, verify amounts, validate customer
Customer tier lookup, fraud scoring, inventory check
Apply routing rules, determine destination, set priority

Each stage publishes to NATS, persists to PostgreSQL, and caches in Redis. Failed events go to a Dead Letter Queue for retry.

synapse-spec-first/
├── asyncapi/              # AsyncAPI 3.0 event specs
├── openapi/               # OpenAPI 3.1 REST specs
├── cmd/
│   ├── synapse/           # Application entry point
│   └── synctl/            # Custom code generator
├── internal/
│   ├── generated/         # Generated from specs
│   │   ├── types.gen.go   # 31 domain types
│   │   ├── server.gen.go  # HTTP interface
│   │   ├── client.gen.go  # HTTP client
│   │   └── events.gen.go  # Event handlers
│   ├── handler/           # HTTP handlers
│   ├── pipeline/          # Watermill pipeline
│   ├── conformance/       # Contract testing
│   └── testutil/          # Testcontainers
└── scripts/               # Diagram generation


vim openapi/components/schemas/orders.yaml


go run ./cmd/synctl


go  ./internal/conformance/...

That's it. Spec changes → regenerate → implement → verify. The spec leads, the code follows.

This project wouldn't be possible without brilliant work from:

  • Chi — Lightweight routing done right
  • NATS — Derek Collison's gift to distributed systems

🤔 "But in the Real World..."

I've heard every objection:

"We don't have time to write specs first."

You don't have time to debug integration issues from undocumented API changes either. Pick your poison.

Not when they generate code. Not when conformance tests fail on drift.

"It's too much overhead."

The overhead is front-loaded. The payoff compounds forever.


git clone https://github.com/copyleftdev/synapse-spec-first.git
synapse-spec-first


make setup


make 
make run

We've included a comprehensive Makefile because developer experience matters:

make 
make generate          
make test-conformance  
make test-pipeline     
make diagrams          
make dev

"We're software engineers. We create worlds. Why wouldn't we try to make them perfect?"

A complete demonstration of specification-driven development using Go, showcasing how to build event-driven systems where the spec is the single source of truth.

This project demonstrates a doc-first development workflow:

  1. Write specifications first (OpenAPI 3.1 + AsyncAPI 3.0)
  2. (custom generator)
  3. (handlers, pipeline stages)
  4. Validate with conformance tests (responses must match specs)
git clone https://github.com/copyleftdev/synapse-spec-first.git
 synapse-spec-first


make setup


make 
make run

This project includes a comprehensive Makefile for a pleasant developer experience:

make
One-time setup for new clones
…

If you've never tried spec-first development, I challenge you:

Build your next API starting with OpenAPI.

  1. Implement to the interface

Then tell me it's not worth it.

Someone once told me, "In a perfect world, that would be great."

We're software engineers.

Why wouldn't we try to make them perfect?

This project is a living demonstration. Fork it. Learn from it. Improve it. And maybe, just maybe, next time someone mentions doc-first development, there will be one fewer blank stare.

20 years of testing taught me this: Quality isn't an afterthought. It's the architecture.

Now go build something beautiful.

items.title: Guide to Integrate AWS Strands Agent with AgentCore Runtime using SDK Integration
items.author: Vimal Venugopal
items.content:
Python 3.10+

AWS account with appropriate permissions

Optional: A container engine (Docker, Finch, or Podman) - only required for local testing and advanced deployment scenarios

There are 2 deployment approaches with AgentCore

For simple agents and minimal functions which can use quick deploy

with Automatic HTTP setup and built in deployment options.

For Complex agents, custom middleware, production systems with full

control over HTTP but more setup required, manual server

configuration

SDK Integration **1. Install and import the package

pip install bedrock-agentcore

from bedrock_agentcore.runtime import BedrockAgentCoreApp
from strands import Agent

app = BedrockAgentCoreApp()
agent = Agent()

@app.entrypoint
def invoke(payload):
    """Process user input and return a response"""
    user_message = payload.get("prompt", "Hello")
    result = agent(user_message)
    return {"result": result.message}

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run()


python my_agent.py

# Test with curl:
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/invocations \
-H "Content-Type: application/json" \
-d '{"prompt": "Hello world!"}'
  1. Choose Your Deployment Method

    Method 1: Starter Toolkit (For quick prototyping)

    your_project_directory/ ├── agent_example.py # Your main agent code

    ├── requirements.txt # Dependencies for your agent

    └── .py # Makes the directory a Python package

    strands-agents

    : The agentcore launch --local command requires a container engine (Docker, Finch, or Podman) for local deployment testing. This step is optional - you can skip directly to agentcore launch for AWS deployment if you don't need local testing.

    Method 2: Manual Deployment with boto3

    For more control over the deployment process:

    Package your code as a container image and push it to ECR Create your agent using CreateAgentRuntime:

items.title: The Future of Venture Capital: Building an Autonomous Analyst with Agno, MCP, and A2A
items.author: Aniket Hingane
items.content:
How I Built a Multi-Agent System That Automates Due Diligence

In this extensive engineering log, I document my journey of building , a serious attempt to automate the intellectual labor of Venture Capital due diligence. By leveraging the Model Context Protocol (MCP) for standardized tool access and an messaging layer, I created a swarm of agents that can research a startup, analyze its market, and synthesize an investment memo—all from a single URL. This isn't just a chatbot; it's a glimpse into the future of "Service-as-Software". You can find the full open-source code here: GitHub - VC-Agent Pro.

I observed recently that the conversation around AI is shifting. We are moving away from "chatting with a bot" and towards "agents doing work." As per my experience in the industry, the biggest bottleneck in high-stakes decision-making—like Venture Capital—is not a lack of data, but the time it takes to synthesize it.

I wrote this because I wanted to test a hypothesis: Can we build a system that doesn't just summarizetext, but actively a business?

In my opinion, the current generation of RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) apps are passive. They wait for you to ask. I wanted to build something active. Something that, once triggered, goes off and on its own, spawning sub-agents to act as specialized analysts, just like a real investment firm would staff a deal team.

From my perspective, the key to this isn't just "better prompts"—it's better architecture. That's why I chose to explore the combination of (for orchestration), (for connecting to the world), and (for agents talking to agents).

What's This Article About?

This article is a deep dive—a really deep dive—into the engineering decisions, architectural patterns, and code required to build a production-grade multi-agent system.

I put it this way coz I want to move beyond the "Hello World" of AI agents. We aren't just calling a weather API here. We are building:

  1. A Protocol-First Architecture: Decoupling the brain (LLM) from the hands (Tools).
  2. A Simulated Enterprise Environment: Where agents define "contracts" to communicate.
  3. : That hides the complexity behind a sleek "AG-UI".

I think this guide will be valuable if you are an engineer looking to understand how the pieces of the "Agentic Stack" fit together in late 2024.

To solve this problem, I selected a stack that prioritizes modularity and standard protocols.

  • : Python (Custom Agent Loop & Agno Concepts). I chose Python because it is the lingua franca of AI.
  • : Model Context Protocol (MCP). This is crucial. Instead of writing custom scrapers for every site, I used MCP to standardize how my agents "see" the world—whether that's searching the web or querying a SQL database.
  • : Protocol using Pydantic. Strictly typed messages ensure our agents don't hallucinate the of the work, even if they get creative with the content.
  • : . For the "AG-UI" (Agent-UI). I needed something that could stream events in real-time to show the user that work is happening.

In my opinion, picking the right stack is 90% of the battle. By using MCP, I future-proofed the application. If I want to switch from Brave Search to Google Search later, I don't change a line of agent code—I just swap the MCP server.

You should read this because the era of "Single Agent" apps is ending. The future is "Agent Swarms".

As per my experience building this, the complexity grows exponentially when you add a second agent. Race conditions, infinite loops, and "telephone" games (where context is lost between agents) are real problems. I observed these failures firsthand so you don't have to.

I documented every step, every failure, and every design decision. If you are serious about building AI that contributes to the real economy, this architecture is a blueprint you can adapt.

Before I wrote a single line of code, I sat down and mapped out the "Cognitive Architecture".

I thought about how a real VC firm works. You have:

  1. ** The Partner (Orchestrator)*: They don't do the grunt work. They know *what needs to be done and should do it. They hold the "State" of the deal.
  2. The Associate (Researcher): They are the diggers. They go to Google, Crunchbase, and GitHub. They bring back raw data.
  3. The Analyst (Market Expert): They look at the numbers. They compare the startup to competitors in the database.

In my design, the is the only one who talks to the User. The user says "Check out Stripe.com". The Orchestrator then creates a and spins up the swarm. It sends a typed to the : "Get me the product breakdown." It sends a message to the : "Who are the competitors?" The sub-agents use to execute these tasks safely. Finally, the Orchestrator aggregates the results into a standardized artifact.

This "Hub and Spoke" model, in my opinion, is superior to a "Chain" model because it allows for parallel execution. The market analysis doesn't need to wait for the product research—they can happen simultaneously.

Now, let's get into the code. This is where the rubber meets the road. I've broken this down into the core components that make the system tick.

1. The A2A Protocol (The Language of Agents)

I realized early on that if agents just talk in English paragraphs, things get messy content-wise. We need structure.

I implemented this strictly. Every communication must wrap in this envelope. This allows us to log every interaction, replay the thought process, and debug exactly why an agent did X. As per my experience, if you don't type your agent comms, you're building a house of cards.

2. The MCP Client (The Hands)

For the agents to interact with the world, I built a simulated MCP client. In a full production deploy, this would connect to a local MCP server process, but for this PoC, I mocked the interface to demonstrate the without requiring you to install 5 different sidecars.

In my opinion, this abstraction is beautiful. The agent doesn't know works. It just knows it exists. This is the power of the Model Context Protocol.

3. The Orchestrator (The Brain)

This is the most complex piece. The Orchestrator manages the state machine.

I wrote this logic to be sequential for clarity, but in a production version (which I've experimented with), I would use to run these steps in parallel. However, strictly sequential logic is easier to debug when you are first learning the A2A patterns.

If you want to run this yourself, I've made it incredibly easy. I bundled everything into a repository structure that separates the "Brain" (Backend) from the "Face" (Frontend).

  1. git clone https://github.com/aniket-work/vc-agent-pro.git
vc-agent-pro

  2. : I kept the requirements minimal so you don't spend hours fighting dependency hell.

    pip  requirements.txt

  3. : Ensure you see the and folders. If you see , you're good to go.

Now for the magic moment. We need two terminal windows.

In Terminal 1 (The Brain): Start the FastAPI server. This spins up the agents and listens for instructions.

uvicorn backend.main:app

You'll see the logs light up: INFO: Uvicorn running on http://127.0.0.1:8000.

: Start the Streamlit AG-UI.

streamlit run frontend/app.py

Navigate to . You will see the "VC-Agent Pro" dashboard. Enter a startup URL (e.g., ) and hit "Start Analysis".

: You will see the "A2A" messages flying back and forth:

[Orchestrator] 🔌 Connecting to MCP Server...[Researcher] Analyzing search results...[MarketAnalyst] Found 3 key competitors...

In my opinion, watching these logs is the most satisfying part. It feels like watching a digital organism think.

Building taught me that the future involved much more than just "chatting". The code I wrote here—using A2A and MCP—demonstrates a pattern where AI can perform complex, multi-step knowledge work that was previously the domain of junior analysts.

I think we are only scratching the surface. Imagine adding a "Legal Agent" that checks for compliance, or a "Finance Agent" that reads uploaded PDFs. With the modular architecture I've laid out, adding those is just a matter of creating a new class and registering it with the Orchestrator.

I observed that the line between "Software" and "Service" is blurring. This tool isn't just software; it's a service. It performs labor. And that is a very exciting, and slightly terrifying, future.

The views and opinions expressed here are solely my own and do not represent the views, positions, or opinions of my employer or any organization I am affiliated with. The content is based on my personal experience and experimentation and may be incomplete or incorrect. Any errors or misinterpretations are unintentional, and I apologize in advance if any statements are misunderstood or misrepresented.

items.title: Cursor CEO Warns Vibe Coding Builds 'Shaky Foundations' That Eventually Crumble
items.author: msmash
items.content:
Michael Truell, the 25-year-old CEO and cofounder of Cursor, is drawing a sharp distinction between careful AI-assisted development and the more hands-off approach commonly known as "vibe coding." Speaking at a conference, Truell described vibe coding as a method where users "close your eyes and you don't look at the code at all and you just ask the AI to go build the thing for you." He compared it to constructing a house by putting up four walls and a roof without understanding the underlying wiring or floorboards. The approach might work for quickly mocking up a game or website, but more advanced projects face real risks. 

"If you close your eyes and you don't look at the code and you have AIs build things with shaky foundations as you add another floor, and another floor, and another floor, and another floor, things start to kind of crumble," Truell said. Truell and three fellow MIT graduates created Cursor in 2022. The tool embeds AI directly into the integrated development environment and uses the context of existing code to predict the next line, generate functions, and debug errors. The difference, as Truell frames it, is that programmers stay engaged with what's happening under the hood rather than flying blind.

items.title: Rethinking Expense Splitting: A Graph-Based Approach with LLM Integration
items.author: Naveen Vandanapu
items.content:
The Problem with Traditional Expense Splitters

If you've ever used group expense splitting tools, you know the drill: great concept, but many implementations leave something to be desired. A common pain point is when timelines mix expenses from different groups, making it difficult to focus on a specific trip or event. Want to quickly review just your weekend getaway expenses? You might find yourself scrolling through unrelated bills and dinner splits from other groups.

This counter-intuitive design pattern got me thinking: there has to be a better way.

A Graph-Based Mental Model

The breakthrough came when I realized expense splitting is fundamentally a . Here's the elegant simplicity:

  • represent people in your group
  • represent money owed (A → B means A owes B)
  • are the amounts

When you add an expense, you're essentially creating edges in this graph. If Alice pays $120 for lunch shared equally among three people, she's owed $40 by Bob and $40 by Charlie. That's two edges: Bob → Alice ($40) and Charlie → Alice ($40). This mental model is intuitive because it mirrors how we naturally think about debts: "I owe Alice $40" is a directed relationship with a value attached.

Building expense-splitter

Armed with this insight, I built expense-splitter as a graph-based tool. Key design decisions:

  1. Group isolation: Each group is its own separate graph. Your Napa trip expenses never mix with your apartment utilities.
  2. Flexible splitting: Support for equal splits, weighted splits, and percentage-based splits—all translating cleanly to edge calculations.
  3. Clean settlements: The graph makes it trivial to calculate who owes whom. No more confusing chains of "Alice pays Bob who pays Charlie"—just direct settlements.

The MCP Integration: Where It Gets Interesting

Here's where modern AI tooling transforms the experience. I packaged expense-splitter as an MCP (Model Context Protocol) tool and built an agent using Google's Agent Development Kit (ADK) in Go. The result? A conversational interface that's actually intuitive:

User: let's do expense splitting

Agent: I can help with that! First, let's create a group.
What would you like to name it?

User: create a group called napa-trip

Agent: Great! I've created a group called 'napa-trip'.
Now, who are the members of this group?

User: add alice, bob, charlie to the group

Agent: I've added Alice, Bob, and Charlie to the napa-trip group.
Now, let's add some expenses...

No clicking through forms. No dropdown menus. Just natural conversation.

Adding Expenses Naturally

The real magic shows when adding expenses:

User: add an expense by alice for lunch. 120$

Agent: I've added a $120 expense for lunch, paid by Alice.
What's the next expense?

User: it's for snacks and drinks by charlie. 60$

Agent: Got it. $60 for snacks and drinks, paid by Charlie, has been added.

Even complex splits feel natural:

User: add an expense by bob for show tickets with unequal weights

Agent: Okay, Bob paid for show tickets with unequal weights.
What was the total amount? And how would you like to split
the weights among Alice, Bob, and Charlie?

User: 100

Agent: And what are the unequal weights for Alice, Bob, and Charlie?

User: alice: 1, bob:2, charlie:1

Agent: I've added the $100 expense for show tickets, paid by Bob,
with the following weights: Alice 1, Bob 2, Charlie 1.

Bob paid $100 but his share (weight 2 out of 4 total) was $50, so he's actually only owed $50.

The Payoff: Clear Settlements

When you're ready to settle up:

User: get group details now

Agent: Here are the details for the 'napa-trip' group:

**Members:** Alice, Bob, Charlie

**Settlement Details:**

- Bob needs to pay Alice $15
- Charlie needs to pay Alice $20
- Charlie needs to pay Bob $5

Visualizing the Graph: DOT Notation

One of the advantages of modeling expenses as a graph is that you can actually see the structure. The tool exports the graph in DOT notation, which can be visualized using Graphviz:

This represents the complete debt graph showing every individual obligation. Notice that this is the raw graph showing all pairwise debts from each expense. The settlement algorithm then simplifies this:

  • Alice owes Bob $25 but Bob owes Alice $40 → Net: Bob owes Alice $15
  • Charlie owes Alice $40 but Alice owes Charlie $20 → Net: Charlie owes Alice $20
  • Charlie owes Bob $25 but Bob owes Charlie $20 → Net: Charlie owes Bob $5

The beauty of the graph representation is its transparency. You can:

  • : Paste into any Graphviz tool to see the network of obligations
  • : See exactly how each expense contributed to the final settlements
  • : Verify the math by tracing through the edges
  • : The visual representation makes the settlement logic obvious

This level of transparency is rare in expense splitting tools. Most are black boxes where you input expenses and get settlements without seeing the underlying logic. By exposing the graph, users who want to understand why they owe a certain amount can trace it back through the structure. Clean, minimal, and derived directly from the graph structure. No intermediate transfers, no confusion.

The combination of a sound data model (graphs) and an intuitive interface (conversational AI) creates something genuinely better than existing solutions:

  1. Cognitive alignment: The graph model matches how we think about debts
  2. Context preservation: Groups stay separate, making it easy to review specific events
  3. Conversational ease: No UI to learn, just describe what you want
  4. Extensibility: MCP tools can be integrated with various AI platforms (Claude Code, custom agents, etc.)

For those interested, the stack is:

: Graph-based settlement calculation implemented in : Exposed as Model Context Protocol tool implemented in : Built with Google's Agent Development Kit - ADK in Go: Natural language via LLM

The MCP abstraction means the same tool can be used from CLI tools like Claude Code, web interfaces, or custom agents—wherever you can connect an LLM.

Sometimes the best solutions come from stepping back and asking: "What's the fundamental structure of this problem?" For expense splitting, it was recognizing the graph nature of debts and obligations. Combined with modern AI tooling, we can finally build interfaces that feel as natural as asking a friend, "Hey, what do I owe you?"

The code is a testament to how good abstractions—both in data modeling and tool interfaces—can transform user experience from frustrating to delightful.

Have you experienced similar frustrations with existing tools? I'd love to hear about problems you've solved by rethinking the fundamental data model.

items.title: Is this the end of "Context Rot"? A first look at the Dropstone Recursive Swarm architecture.
items.author: Temp-insta
items.content:
I don't work for the team at Blankline, but I’ve been following their recent whitepapers on "Linearity Barriers". I finally got to test the Dropstone v2 runtime, and I think we need to talk about what’s happening here.

We are currently seeing a lot of "AI Washing"—products that claim to be "General Intelligence" but are just basic wrappers. Dropstone is different. It doesn't claim to be a "God Model"; it's a fundamental rethink of how an AI agent navigates a codebase.

The "Superhuman" Capability (Wait, hear me out)

I usually hate using the word "superhuman," but there is a specific metric where this topology moves past what a single human (or a single LLM) can do: Parallel Trajectory Search.

When you give Dropstone a task, it doesn't just guess a solution. It spawns a "Recursive Swarm"—effectively thousands of scout agents—to explore divergent timelines in the background.

Why this isn't just another wrapper:

  1. Autonomous Entropy Pruning: Instead of filling your context window with every thought, it calculates "uncertainty" (semantic entropy). If a scout agent gets confused, that entire timeline is killed before it hits your history.

  2. Stable Long-Horizon Engineering: I watched this thing handle a complex refactor that took nearly 24 hours of autonomous runtime. Usually, an agent would have "dementia" by hour two. This didn't.

Check out the architecture breakdown from the founder:

// Detect dark theme var iframe = document.getElementById('tweet-2004349561775837406-401'); if (document.body.className.includes('dark-theme')) { iframe.src = "https://platform.twitter.com/embed/Tweet.html?id=2004349561775837406&theme=dark" }

We aren't at AGI yet, but we are entering a phase of High-Competence Agentic Runtimes. If you are tired of your agents looping and apologizing, the "Recursive Swarm" approach might be the only way forward for enterprise-grade AI engineering.

items.title: Apple's App Course Runs $20,000 a Student. Is It Really Worth It?
items.author: msmash
items.content:
Apple's Developer Academy in Detroit has spent roughly $30 million over four years training hundreds of people to build iPhone apps, but not everyone lands coding jobs right away, according to a WIRED story published this week. 

The program launched in 2021 as part of Apple's $200 million response to the Black Lives Matter protests and costs an estimated $20,000 per student -- nearly twice what state and local governments budget for community colleges. About 600 students have completed the 10-month course at Michigan State University. Academy officials say 71% of graduates from the past two years found full-time jobs across various industries. 

The program provides iPhones, MacBooks and stipends ranging from $800 to $1,500 per month, though one former student said many participants relied on food stamps. Apple contributed $11.6 million to the academy. Michigan taxpayers and the university's regular students covered about $8.6 million -- nearly 30% of total funding. Two graduates said their lack of proficiency in Android hurt their job prospects. Apple's own US tech workforce went from 6% Black before the academy opened to about 3% this year.

items.title: TinyWorkflow: A Lightweight Python Library for Learning Workflow Orchestration
items.author: Sai Kumar Yava
items.content:
I built TinyWorkflow to solve a problem I kept encountering: developers wanting to learn workflow orchestration concepts without the complexity of production-grade infrastructure. After watching colleagues struggle with setup complexity when they just wanted to experiment with workflow patterns, I decided to create something different.

TinyWorkflow is a Python-first workflow library designed specifically for learning, prototyping, and lightweight automation. It's ~2,000 lines of readable code that you can understand in an afternoon.

TinyWorkflow is a workflow orchestration library that lets you:

  • using Python decorators
  • with automatic retry logic
  • with fan-out/fan-in patterns
  • to SQLite, PostgreSQL, or MySQL
  • with cron expressions
  • through a built-in web UI
  • with exponential backoff

All without external services, Docker containers, or complex configurations.

Here's a complete workflow in TinyWorkflow:

Installation is straightforward:

pip tinyworkflow
python your_workflow.py

No additional setup required.

TinyWorkflow uses Python decorators to define workflows and activities. No YAML, no XML, no DSL. Just Python functions:

2. Automatic State Persistence

Every workflow execution is automatically persisted to a database. TinyWorkflow supports:

  • (default, zero configuration)
  • (for team environments)
  • (if that's your preference)

State persistence includes:

  • Workflow inputs and outputs
  • Activity execution results
  • Event logs (complete audit trail)
  • Retry attempts and failure reasons

3. Retry Logic with Exponential Backoff

Activities can automatically retry on failure with configurable backoff:

The retry system includes:

  • Jitter to prevent thundering herd
  • Configurable delay limits
  • Per-activity timeout settings

Execute multiple activities concurrently using the parallel execution pattern:

This is useful when activities are independent and can run concurrently.

5. Human-in-the-Loop Workflows

Workflows can pause and wait for human approval:

Approvals can be managed through the CLI or web UI.

Schedule workflows to run on a cron schedule:

Or schedule one-time delayed execution:

TinyWorkflow includes a web interface for monitoring and management:

tinyworkflow server  your_module.workflows  8080
  • Dashboard with workflow statistics
  • List of all workflow executions
  • Detailed event logs for each workflow
  • Ability to start new workflows
  • Approval queue for human-in-the-loop workflows
  • Registry of available workflows and activities

Built with FastAPI, the UI is fast and can be extended if needed.

8. Command-Line Interface

Manage workflows from the terminal:


tinyworkflow start my_workflow 
tinyworkflow status <run_id>


tinyworkflow list


tinyworkflow events <run_id>


tinyworkflow schedule my_workflow 
tinyworkflow approve <run_id> 
tinyworkflow server  examples.workflows

Every state change is recorded as an event, providing a complete audit trail:

  • Debugging workflow failures
  • Understanding execution history
  • Compliance and audit requirements
  • Analyzing workflow performance

TinyWorkflow is well-suited for multi-step AI pipelines:

This pattern is useful for:

  • Content generation and moderation
  • Document processing and analysis
  • Multi-step model inference
  • Prompt experimentation and testing

Data Processing Pipelines

Build ETL workflows with automatic retries:

Run periodic tasks with cron scheduling:

Implement business processes requiring human intervention:

TinyWorkflow is built with specific goals:

The codebase is intentionally small (~2,000 lines) and readable. You can:

  • Read the entire source code to understand implementation
  • Learn workflow patterns through clear examples
  • Experiment without infrastructure overhead
  • Understand trade-offs in workflow design

TinyWorkflow requires only:

  • SQLAlchemy (database abstraction)
  • APScheduler (cron scheduling)
  • Standard async/await libraries

No external services, containers, or orchestrators needed.

3. Database-Centric State Management

State is persisted to a database (SQLite by default). This means:

  • No external state management service
  • Simple backup and recovery (database dumps)
  • Easy migration between databases
  • Familiar tools for debugging (SQL queries)

4. Progressive Disclosure

Start simple, add complexity as needed:

  • Basic workflow: Just activities in sequence
  • Add retries: Configure RetryPolicy
  • Add parallelism: Use execute_parallel
  • Add approvals: Use wait_for_approval
  • Add scheduling: Use cron expressions

Understanding the Limitations

TinyWorkflow makes specific trade-offs for simplicity. Here's what it doesn't provide:

If a workflow fails, it restarts from the beginning, not from the failure point. This is acceptable for:

  • Short workflows (under 30 minutes)
  • Workflows where activities are idempotent
  • Learning and experimentation scenarios

This limitation exists because implementing replay would require:

  • Deterministic execution constraints
  • Significantly increased codebase complexity

No Deterministic Execution

You can use , , and freely in workflows. This provides flexibility but means workflows may produce different results on retry.

Using in workflows means timer state is lost if the process crashes. For production systems requiring durable timers, use external schedulers or production-grade orchestrators.

Running workflows cannot receive external events or signals. Communication with running workflows is limited to the approval mechanism.

No Automatic Compensation

There are no saga patterns or automatic rollback. If you need compensation logic, implement it explicitly in your activities.

TinyWorkflow is tested and works well with:

  • Up to 100 concurrent workflows
  • Workflows under 1 hour duration
  • Moderate throughput (not high-frequency trading)

For higher scales, production-grade orchestrators are more appropriate.

  • Understanding workflow orchestration concepts
  • Teaching in courses or workshops
  • Experimenting with workflow patterns
  • Reading source code to learn implementation
  • Testing workflow ideas quickly
  • Building MVPs without infrastructure
  • Weekend projects and hackathons
  • Proof-of-concept implementations
  • Testing different prompts or models
  • Document processing workflows
  • Content generation and moderation
  • Scheduled data pipelines (< 1 hour)
  • Internal tools and scripts
  • Personal automation projects
  • Small-scale document processing

Production-Critical Systems

  • Systems where downtime is costly
  • Workflows requiring guaranteed execution
  • Long-running processes (multiple hours/days)
  • High-scale data processing (TB+)

Complex Enterprise Requirements

  • Advanced workflow patterns (sagas, compensation)
  • Strict compliance and audit requirements
  • High-availability guarantees
  • Enterprise support contracts

The repository includes 20 example workflows:

python examples/approval_workflow.py

Open to explore the interface.

Build Your First Workflow

Create a file :

Documentation and Resources

  • Complete API documentation
  • Workflow registration guide
  • Limitations documentation
  • GitHub Issues for bug reports
  • GitHub Discussions for questions
  • Example code in the repository

I built TinyWorkflow with a few principles:

The entire codebase is ~2,000 lines. This isn't a limitation—it's intentional. Every decision favors clarity over cleverness.

The LIMITATIONS.md file explicitly documents what TinyWorkflow doesn't do and why. This helps users make informed decisions.

All 20 example workflows are complete, working code that demonstrates actual use cases, not toy problems.

Examples start simple and gradually introduce more complex patterns:

  1. Simple sequential workflow

TinyWorkflow is open source (MIT License) and welcomes contributions:

  • Report bugs or suggest features (GitHub Issues)
  • Submit pull requests with improvements
  • Share your use cases and examples
  • Write tutorials or blog posts
git clone https://github.com/scionoftech/tinyworkflow

pytest tests/

Future development focuses on:

  • More example workflows from real use cases
  • Better error messages and debugging tools
  • Integration guides for popular libraries
  • Performance optimizations

TinyWorkflow will remain focused on its core mission: making workflow orchestration concepts accessible through hands-on experimentation.

TinyWorkflow is a learning-focused workflow library that prioritizes simplicity and readability. It provides the core workflow patterns—activities, retries, parallel execution, state persistence—without the complexity of production-grade orchestrators.

If you're learning workflow orchestration, prototyping AI pipelines, or building lightweight automation, TinyWorkflow offers a straightforward path to getting started.

If you build something with TinyWorkflow, I'd love to hear about it. Open an issue or start a discussion on GitHub.

MIT 3.9+ Active development, open to contributions

items.title: GPU Compute Shaders in Pure Go: gogpu/gg v0.15.1
items.author: Andrey Kolkov
items.content:
GPU Compute Shaders in Pure Go: gogpu/gg v0.15.1

Two days ago, we shipped gogpu/gg v0.14.0 with alpha masks and fluent PathBuilder. Yes, two days — we're moving fast. But looking at performance profiles, I saw a problem.

The CPU was the bottleneck.

While our GPU could render millions of pixels in milliseconds, the CPU spent significant time tessellating paths. This is the classic 2D graphics problem: CPU tessellation doesn't scale.

So we moved the entire rasterization pipeline to GPU compute shaders.

Today, is here: 2,280 lines of WGSL compute shaders, vello-style pipeline, dramatic speedups for complex scenes. All in Pure Go.

The Performance Challenge

Let me show you what we were up against:

The problem with traditional 2D rendering:

  • CPU tessellates paths to triangles (sequential, single-threaded)
  • GPU renders triangles (massively parallel)
  • CPU becomes the bottleneck for complex scenes

This is true for traditional 2D library (Cairo, Skia, even our earlier versions).

The Solution: All-GPU Pipeline

What if we never tessellated on CPU?

Traditional: Path → Tessellate (CPU) → Triangles → GPU
     v0.15: Path → Flatten (GPU) → Coarse (GPU) → Fine (GPU)

This is the vello approach. We implemented it in Pure Go with WGSL compute shaders:

1. Flatten Shader — Curves to Segments

Converts Bezier curves to line segments :

@compute @workgroup_size(256)
fn flatten_main(
    @builtin(global_invocation_id) id: vec3<u32>
) {
    let curve = curves[id.x];

    // Adaptive subdivision based on curvature
    let segments = subdivide_bezier(curve);

    // Write to global buffer (thousands in parallel!)
    for (var i = 0u; i < segment_count; i++) {
        output_segments[base + i] = segments[i];
    }
}

Thousands of GPU cores process curves simultaneously. CPU does one at a time.

2. Coarse Shader — Tile Binning

Maps segments to screen tiles using (thread-safe on GPU!):

@compute @workgroup_size(256)
fn coarse_main(
    @builtin(global_invocation_id) id: vec3<u32>
) {
    let segment = segments[id.x];
    let bounds = segment_bounds(segment);

    // Find overlapping tiles
    for (var y = tile_min.y; y <= tile_max.y; y++) {
        for (var x = tile_min.x; x <= tile_max.x; x++) {
            let tile_id = y * tile_width + x;

            // Atomic append (safe across threads!)
            let index = atomicAdd(&tile_counts[tile_id], 1u);
            tile_segments[tile_id][index] = id.x;
        }
    }
}

This is where GPU shines — thousands of threads binning segments in parallel, atomics keeping data consistent.

3. Fine Shader — Per-Pixel Coverage

Computes anti-aliased coverage (no MSAA needed!):

@compute @workgroup_size(8, 8)
fn fine_main(
    @builtin(global_invocation_id) pixel: vec3<u32>
) {
    let tile_id = (pixel.y / TILE_SIZE) * tile_width + (pixel.x / TILE_SIZE);

    var coverage = 0.0;
    for (var i = 0u; i < tile_counts[tile_id]; i++) {
        let seg = segments[tile_segments[tile_id][i]];
        coverage += compute_segment_coverage(seg, pixel.xy);
    }

    // Apply fill rule, write pixel
    output[pixel.xy] = vec4<f32>(color.rgb, saturate(coverage));
}

Perfect anti-aliasing at scale. No jaggies, no MSAA overhead.

Expected Performance Gains

Based on our architecture and GPU parallelism theory, we expect significant improvements for complex scenes:

Simple paths (< 100 segments)CPU may be faster (GPU upload overhead)
Medium complexity (100-1K segments)
Complex scenes (1K-10K segments)GPU significantly faster (10-30x estimated)
Very complex (10K+ segments)GPU dominates (30-60x estimated)

These are architectural projections. Actual performance varies by hardware, driver, and scene complexity. We're working on comprehensive benchmarks.

The larger the scene, the bigger the advantage. This is parallelism at work.

Hybrid Architecture: Best of Both Worlds

Here's the clever part — we don't use GPU:

Small paths (< 100 segments by default) have GPU dispatch overhead. CPU wins for simple cases.

Automatic selection based on segment count.

Challenge 1: WGSL Compilation

WGSL needs to compile to SPIR-V for Vulkan, MSL for Metal. We use our own gogpu/naga compiler (~23K LOC Pure Go):

Supports vertex, fragment, compute shaders. Atomics, barriers, texture sampling.

Challenge 2: GPU Memory Management

Buffer sizing is critical:

We use conservative estimates with safety margins. Real-world paths rarely exceed our allocations.

Challenge 3: Atomic Operations

  • Only and supported
  • Specific memory orders required
// This works
@group(0) @binding(1) var<storage, read_write> counts: array<atomic<u32>>;
atomicAdd(&counts[i], 1u);

// This doesn't
var<storage, read_write> counts: array<u32>; // Not atomic!

Debugging this was tricky. WGSL validation errors are... cryptic.

  • (8 shader files)
  • in backend/wgpu/
backend/wgpu/shaders/
├── flatten.wgsl     # 589 LOC — Bezier curve flattening
├── coarse.wgsl      # 335 LOC — Tile binning with atomics
├── fine.wgsl        # 290 LOC — Per-pixel coverage
├── blend.wgsl       # 424 LOC — 29 blend modes on GPU
├── composite.wgsl   # 235 LOC — Layer compositing
├── strip.wgsl       # 155 LOC — Sparse strip rendering
├── blit.wgsl        #  43 LOC — Final output blit
└── msdf_text.wgsl   # 209 LOC — MSDF text rendering

backend/wgpu/
├── gpu_flatten.go       # 809 LOC — Flatten pipeline
├── gpu_coarse.go        # 698 LOC — Coarse rasterization
├── gpu_fine.go          # 752 LOC — Fine rasterization
├── sparse_strips_gpu.go # 837 LOC — Hybrid CPU/GPU selection
├── renderer.go          # 822 LOC — Main renderer
├── pipeline.go          # 369 LOC — Pipeline orchestration
├── memory.go            # 413 LOC — GPU memory management
└── ... (40+ files total)

go get github.com/gogpu/gg@v0.15.1

The library automatically selects the best backend. GPU is used by default when available:


go build ./...


go build  nogpu ./...

With v0.15.1, the GoGPU ecosystem now totals:

Total: ~224K lines of Pure Go. No CGO. No Rust. Just .

We're the only Pure Go library with GPU compute shaders for 2D graphics.

Entering the Stabilization Phase

With v0.15.1, we're closing the rapid development chapter.

In just three weeks, gogpu/gg went from v0.1.0 to v0.15.1 — from a basic context to GPU compute shaders. We've built the foundation: software rasterizer, GPU backend, text rendering, scene graph, blend modes, and now compute shaders.

What comes next is different. We're shifting focus to:

  • — Bug fixes, edge cases, cross-platform testing
  • — Real benchmarks, profiling, optimization
  • — API docs, tutorials, examples
  • — Better error messages, developer experience

This is where becomes critical. We've built the architecture — now we need real-world usage to find the rough edges.

  1. — Try gogpu/gg in your projects, report issues
  2. — Run performance tests on your hardware
  3. — Find edge cases, corner cases, crashes
  4. — Examples, tutorials, use cases 
go get github.com/gogpu/gg@v0.15.1

v0.16.0 — v0.19.0 (Stabilization):

  • Comprehensive benchmark suite
  • Performance profiling and optimization
  • Bug fixes from community feedback
  • Documentation and examples

v1.0.0 (Production Ready):

    This work was inspired by:

    Special thanks to the Go community for the continued support and feedback!

    From CPU bottleneck to GPU parallelism. From sequential tessellation to massively parallel compute shaders.

    This is what Pure Go can do.

    go get github.com/gogpu/gg@v0.15.1

    Star the repo if you find it useful!

    Part of the GoGPU Journey series:

items.title: Mastering HTTP/2 in Go: A Practical Guide for Building Faster Web Servers
items.author: Jones Charles
items.content:
Hey, Go developers! 🚀 If you’re building web servers and want to supercharge their performance, HTTP/2 is your ticket to the fast lane. Compared to HTTP/1.1’s single-lane road, HTTP/2 is like a multi-lane highway, slashing latency and handling concurrency like a dream. Pair it with Go’s lightweight goroutines, and you’ve got a recipe for blazing-fast APIs and apps.

This guide is for developers with 1-2 years of Go experience who know the basics of . We’ll walk through HTTP/2’s core features, show you how to implement them in Go, and share optimization tips from real-world projects like e-commerce APIs and real-time dashboards. Whether you’re speeding up a single-page app (SPA) or scaling a high-traffic API, you’ll leave with actionable insights. Let’s dive in! 🏄‍♂️

What’s HTTP/2, and Why Should You Care?

HTTP/2 (released in 2015, RFC 7540) is a major upgrade over HTTP/1.1, tackling its biggest pain points: head-of-line blocking, bloated headers, and limited concurrency. Here’s what makes HTTP/2 awesome:

  • : Send multiple requests over a single TCP connection, like streaming Netflix while browsing Twitter—no waiting in line!
  • Header Compression (HPACK): Shrinks headers to save bandwidth, like zipping repetitive metadata.
  • : Proactively sends resources (e.g., CSS/JS) to the client, cutting load times.
  • : Tells the server which resources matter most, so critical content loads first.

Here’s a quick comparison:

One request per TCP (or limited pipelining)Single connection, multiple streams
Yes, proactive resource delivery
Fine-grained stream control

Table 1: HTTP/1.1 vs. HTTP/2 at a Glance

Go’s package has supported HTTP/2 since version 1.6—no external libraries needed! Combine that with Go’s goroutines, and you get:

  • : Multiplexing cuts connection overhead, perfect for high-traffic apps.
  • : Goroutines handle streams effortlessly, making concurrency a breeze.
  • : HTTP/2 plays nice with tools like gRPC for advanced use cases.

In a recent e-commerce API project, switching to HTTP/2 slashed response times from 300ms to 210ms—a 30% boost! Go’s concurrency made handling thousands of requests feel like a walk in the park. 😎

Have you played with HTTP/2 in Go yet? Drop a comment below and share your experience! Next, let’s get hands-on with some code to bring HTTP/2 to life.

Getting Started with HTTP/2 in Go

Go makes HTTP/2 a breeze with its package. Whether you’re testing locally with h2c (HTTP/2 over cleartext) or deploying with TLS, setting up an HTTP/2 server is straightforward. Let’s walk through a basic server, add server push, and see it in action with an e-commerce example.

Building a Basic HTTP/2 Server

Go negotiates HTTP/2 automatically via ALPN (Application-Layer Protocol Negotiation) when using TLS. Here’s a simple TLS-enabled server:

  • : Routes incoming requests.
  • : Enables TLS and auto-negotiates HTTP/2.
  • : Generate and with OpenSSL or use a tool like for local testing.

For development, you can use h2c (no TLS) with the package:

: Use or Chrome DevTools (look for in the protocol column) to confirm HTTP/2 is active.

Server push lets you send resources before the client asks, like preloading CSS for an SPA. Go’s interface makes this easy:

  • : Checks if the client supports push.
  • : Sends to the client’s cache, speeding up rendering.
  • : Log errors to debug push issues (e.g., unsupported clients).

In an e-commerce project, we used server push to preload a 10KB critical CSS file for product pages. This cut first render time from 1.2s to 1s—a 15% win! Chrome DevTools showed pushed resources as . Early on, we pushed large images by mistake, wasting bandwidth. Lesson learned: analyze user behavior and push only what’s critical.

Have you tried server push in your projects? How do you pick resources to push? Share in the comments! Next, let’s optimize HTTP/2 to squeeze out every ounce of performance.

Turbocharging HTTP/2 Performance

HTTP/2 is fast out of the box, but with a few tweaks, you can make it scream. Let’s optimize multiplexing, headers, prioritization, and TLS, with lessons from real projects to guide us.

Multiplexing lets multiple streams flow over one TCP connection, and Go’s goroutines handle them like a champ. But slow handlers or too many streams can clog the pipeline.

  • : Offload heavy tasks (e.g., DB queries) to goroutines.
  • : Use to avoid server overload.

: In a social media API, unlimited streams caused memory spikes during peak traffic. Setting to 50 and using for goroutine control kept things stable.

Shrinking Headers with HPACK

HPACK compresses headers to save bandwidth, like turning verbose labels into compact indices.

  • Stick to Standard Headers: Use over custom headers for better compression.
  • : Avoid unnecessary headers to reduce overhead.

: In one project, custom headers like hurt HPACK efficiency. Switching to standard headers saved 10% bandwidth.

HTTP/2 lets clients prioritize streams, ensuring critical resources (e.g., data over images) load first. Go relies on client hints, but you can tweak server behavior.

: In a real-time stock dashboard, images loaded before data, adding 200ms latency. Adjusting client priorities via JavaScript fixed it.

  • : CSS/JS for rendering.
  • : HTML content.
  • : Images, fonts.

HTTP/2 requires TLS in production, and TLS 1.3 is your best friend for speed and security.

  • Use TLS 1.3, disable older versions (TLS 1.0/1.1).
  • Choose fast cipher suites like .

: In a real-time analytics API, TLS 1.3 and optimized ciphers cut connection time by 20% and latency by 15%.

Have you hit TLS snags with HTTP/2? How do you balance speed and security? Drop a comment below!

HTTP/2 is powerful, but it’s not perfect. Here are common gotchas and how to dodge them, based on real-world experience.

Pitfall 1: Overusing Server Push

: Pushing too many resources (e.g., huge images) wastes bandwidth.

  • Push only critical assets like CSS/JS.
  • Use DevTools to check push effectiveness.

: In an e-commerce app, pushing all product images spiked bandwidth by 20%. Focusing on CSS/JS and caching cut usage by 10%.

Pitfall 2: Client Compatibility

: Older clients may not support HTTP/2, breaking functionality.

  • Rely on ’s ALPN for HTTP/2 and HTTP/1.1 fallback.
  • Test with tools like or older browsers.

: Some low-version clients failed to load resources. Adding protocol logging and fallback fixed it.

Pitfall 3: Resource Overload

: Too many streams under high traffic can crash your server.

  • Set and .
  • Limit streams with .

: In a social media API, timeouts and stream limits reduced downtime by 90% during traffic spikes.

Real-World Success Stories

HTTP/2 shines in Go projects. Here are three examples:

  1. (Social Media)

    • Used multiplexing and goroutines to handle thousands of requests.
    • Set to 100.
    • : Response time dropped 30% (300ms to 210ms).

  2. (Finance)

    • Streamed stock data with prioritized streams.
    • Simplified architecture with HTTP/2.
    • : Update latency fell from 500ms to 350ms.
    • Pushed critical CSS/JS with server push.
    • Used HPACK for header compression.
    • : Load time cut by 15% (1.5s to 1.3s).

How has HTTP/2 helped your projects? Share below!

Best Practices Cheat Sheet

  • : Boost speed and security.
  • : Pair with multiplexing for concurrency.
  • : Use to stay stable.
  • : Only send critical resources.
  • : Ensure HTTP/1.1 compatibility.

HTTP/2 and Go are a match made in performance heaven. From multiplexing to server push, you can build lightning-fast servers with minimal effort. Start small with h2c for testing, then go all-in with TLS in production. The Go community on Dev.to is awesome—share your HTTP/2 wins, challenges, or questions in the comments! Let’s learn from each other. 🚀

: Tried HTTP/2 in Go? Drop your story below or share your project on GitHub!

items.title: **Empowering Seniors: A Comprehensive Guide to Setting Up a Smartphone for Elderly Loved Ones**

items.content:
Empowering Seniors: A Comprehensive Guide to Setting Up a Smartphone for Elderly Loved Ones

As technology continues to advance at an unprecedented rate, it's becoming increasingly essential for seniors to stay connected with family and friends, access essential services, and maintain their independence. However, navigating the complexities of modern smartphones can be daunting, especially for those who are not tech-savvy. In this article, we'll provide a step-by-step guide on how to set up a smartphone for elderly loved ones, making it easier for them to use and enjoy the benefits of mobile technology.

Choosing the Right Device

When selecting a smartphone for an elderly loved one, consider the following factors:

  • : Opt for a device with a simple, intuitive interface and a large display.
  • : Look for devices with built-in accessibility features, such as text-to-speech, voice-to-text, and high contrast mode.
  • : Choose a device with a sturdy design and a protective case to withstand accidental drops.

Once you've chosen the right device, follow these steps to set it up:

  1. : Follow the manufacturer's instructions to activate the device and set up the SIM card or eSIM.
  2. : Customize the home screen to display essential apps and features, such as the phone, contacts, and messaging apps.
  3. Configure accessibility settings: Enable accessibility features, such as text-to-speech, voice-to-text, and high contrast mode, to make the device more user-friendly.
  4. : Install apps that are essential for daily life, such as a calendar, reminders, and a medication reminder.

To make the most of their smartphone, seniors can follow these tips:

  • : Consider using a launcher that simplifies the interface and provides easy access to essential apps.
  • : Take advantage of voice commands to make calls, send messages, and access apps.
  • : Consider using a stylus to make it easier to navigate the device and access apps.

Setting up a smartphone for elderly loved ones can be a daunting task, but with the right guidance, it can be a rewarding experience. By following the steps outlined in this article, you can empower seniors to stay connected, access essential services, and maintain their independence. Remember to choose a device that is easy to use, accessible, and durable, and to configure the device to meet their specific needs. With the right setup and support, seniors can enjoy the benefits of mobile technology and stay connected with loved ones.

items.title: **The Right to Repair: A Battle for Control in the Tech Industry**
items.author: Insights YRS
items.content:
The Right to Repair: A Battle for Control in the Tech Industry

The right-to-repair movement has been gaining momentum in recent years, with a growing number of consumers and manufacturers advocating for the ability to repair and maintain their own devices. However, the companies controlling the production and distribution of these devices are not willing to give up their power without a fight. In this article, we will explore the current state of the right-to-repair movement and the challenges that lie ahead.

A Boost from Unlikely Sources

In 2022, the right-to-repair movement received a significant boost from several unlikely sources. The ongoing trade tensions between the US and China led to the imposition of tariffs on imported electronics, making it more expensive for manufacturers to repair devices rather than replace them. Additionally, the economic downturn caused by the pandemic led to a surge in demand for repair services, as consumers looked for ways to extend the life of their devices.

Big Tech's Surprising Support

In a surprising turn of events, several big tech companies, including Apple and Google, began to show support for the right-to-repair movement. Apple, in particular, has been a vocal advocate for the right to repair, releasing repair guides and diagnostic tools for its devices. Google has also taken steps to make its devices more repairable, including the release of a repair manual for its Pixel smartphones.

The Companies Fighting Back

Despite the growing support for the right-to-repair movement, the companies controlling the production and distribution of devices are not giving up their power without a fight. Many manufacturers have implemented complex repair processes and proprietary software that make it difficult for consumers to repair their own devices. Additionally, some companies have been accused of using repair restrictions as a way to lock customers into their ecosystem and prevent them from switching to competing products.

The Future of the Right to Repair

As the right-to-repair movement continues to gain momentum, it is likely that the battle for control in the tech industry will only intensify. While some companies are beginning to show support for the movement, others are digging in their heels and fighting to maintain their control over the repair process. Ultimately, the outcome of this battle will depend on the actions of consumers, manufacturers, and policymakers.

The right to repair is a complex issue that affects not only consumers but also manufacturers and policymakers. As the tech industry continues to evolve, it is essential that we prioritize the ability of consumers to repair and maintain their own devices. By doing so, we can reduce electronic waste, promote sustainability, and give consumers more control over their technology.

  • The right-to-repair movement has received a boost from tariffs, economic downturn, and big tech's support.
  • Companies controlling the production and distribution of devices are fighting back against the movement.
  • The outcome of the battle for control in the tech industry will depend on the actions of consumers, manufacturers, and policymakers.
  • Prioritizing the right to repair can promote sustainability, reduce electronic waste, and give consumers more control over their technology.

SENTINEL is a security platform for LLMs, AI agents, and multimodal systems:

99 detection engines (<10ms latency)

LLM Top 10 ✅ + Agentic Top 10 2026 ✅

Think of it as a , but for AI.

The "Strange Math" Engines

While most AI security tools use pattern matching, we went a different way:

Topological Data Analysis (TDA)

Jailbreak attempts create "holes" in the embedding topology that normal text doesn't.

Coherence verification across multi-turn conversations. Detects attacks that slowly shift context across messages.

Social engineering via AI

items.title: When I Took Numba to the Dojo: A Battle Royale Against Rust and CUDA
items.author: Mr. 0x1
items.content:
His piece captured something beautiful about computing: the joy of experimentation, the thrill of watching code go , and the curiosity to ask "what if?"

"Somewhere between algorithms and hardware, Numba didn't just make my code faster. It made exploration lighter."

Reading his benchmarks, I couldn't help but wonder: What happens when we throw Rust into the mix? What about raw CUDA? Where does the hardware actually give up?

So I built a dojo. Let's spar.

Same challenge as Shreyan's original experiment:

f(x) = sqrt(x² + 1) × sin(x) + cos(x/2)

Compute this for .

Simple math. Maximum optimization.

I assembled fighters from different worlds:

  • — The baseline. Interpreter overhead. GIL-bound.
  • — The standard approach.
  • — Single-threaded compiled.
  • — Multi-threaded with .
  • — Idiomatic iterators.
  • — Work-stealing parallelism.
  • — Cache-optimized chunking.
  • — Python on the GPU.
  • — Double precision native.
  • — Single precision native.
  • — Hardware-optimized math.

I wanted this to be :

  • Same computation across all implementations
  • Same array size (20 million float64 elements)
  • Same random seed (42, obviously)
  • Multiple warmup runs to eliminate JIT/cache effects
  • Take the of multiple runs (least noise)

git clone https://github.com/copyleftdev/numba-dojo.git
numba-dojo
make all

Let's see who survived the dojo.

1. GPU Demolishes CPU (When It Fits)

The RTX 3080 Ti at is than NumPy. That's not a typo.

For embarrassingly parallel workloads like element-wise computation, GPUs are in a different league. The massive parallelism (80 streaming multiprocessors, thousands of cores) absolutely crushes sequential execution.

2. FP32 is 20x Faster Than FP64 on Consumer GPUs 

CUDA FP64: 4.11 ms
CUDA FP32: 0.21 ms  ← 20x faster!

Consumer GPUs (GeForce series) have limited FP64 units — typically 1/32 the throughput of FP32. If your computation can tolerate single precision, .

3. Rust ≈ Numba JIT (Single-Threaded) 

Rust Single:  555.62 ms
Numba JIT:    558.30 ms

Both compile to LLVM IR. Both get similar codegen. The difference is noise. This validates Numba's claim: "Feels like Python, behaves like C."

4. Rust Beats Numba in Parallel (~20%) 

Rust Parallel (Rayon):  12.39 ms
Numba Parallel:         15.55 ms

Rayon's work-stealing scheduler has lower overhead than Numba's threading. For CPU-parallel workloads in production, Rust has an edge.

5. We Hit the Memory Bandwidth Wall

This was the most interesting discovery.

When I profiled the FP32 CUDA kernel:

Time:       0.21 ms
Bandwidth:  ~777 GB/s achieved
Theoretical: 912 GB/s (RTX 3080 Ti)
Efficiency: 85%

We're running at 85% of peak memory bandwidth. The GPU cores are actually . No algorithm can beat physics.

                    Peak Compute
                         /
                        /
Performance            /
                      /  ← We're here (memory-bound)
                     /
                    /
            ──────────────────────
                Memory Bandwidth

For this workload with low arithmetic intensity (few ops per byte), we've hit the ceiling.

Here's what each implementation looks like:

Numba (The Hero of The Original Article)

Just add . That's it. Shreyan was right — this is magical.

Rayon makes parallelism feel as natural as iterators.

Grid-stride loops for maximum occupancy.

NumPy (it's fine, really)
Need 10-50x speedup, stay in Python
Maximum performance, GPU available

Shreyan's original article reminded me why I love computing: we get to ask "what if?" and then actually find out.

What if we compile this loop? What if we use all CPU cores? What if we throw a GPU at it? What if we hit the memory bandwidth wall?

The journey from Pure Python (6.6 seconds) to CUDA FP32 (0.2 milliseconds) is a . That's not optimization — that's transformation.

Keep experimenting. Keep playing. That's what computing is for. ✨

What's your favorite performance optimization story? Drop it in the comments!

items.title: Seth Michael Larson: Getting started with Playdate on Ubuntu 🟨
items.content:
Trina got me a Playdate for Christmas this year! I've always been intrigued by this console, as it is highly constrained in terms of pixel and color-depth (400x240, 2 colors), but also provides many helpful resources for game development such as a software development kit (SDK) and a simulator to quickly test games during development.

I first discovered software programming as an amateur game developer using BYOND, so “returning to my roots” and doing some game development feels like a fun and fulfilling diversion from the current direction software is taking. Plus, I now have a reason to learn a new programming language: Lua!

Getting started with Playdate on Ubuntu

Here's what I did to quickly get started with a Playdate development environment on my Ubuntu 24.04 laptop:

  • Unbox the Playdate and start charging the console so it's charged enough for the next steps involving the console.
  • Download the SDK. For Linux you need to extract to your desired directory (I chose ) and run the setup script (sudo ~/PlaydateSDK/setup.sh).
  • Add the SDK to and environment variables to your .
  • Start the simulator () and register the simulator to your Playdate account when prompted.
  • Turn on the console and play the startup tutorial. Connect to Wi-Fi and let the console update.
  • When prompted by the console, register the console to your Playdate account.
  • Download and install VSCode. I used the installer for Ubuntu.
  • Disable all AI features in VSCode by adding "chat.disableAIFeatures": true to your .
  • Select "Extensions" in VSCode and install the "Lua" and "Playdate Debug" extensions.
  • Create two directories: and . Within the directory create a file called . This file will be the entry-point into your Playdate application.

That's it, your Playdate development environment should be ready to use.

“Hello, world” on the Playdate

Within put the following Lua code:

Try building and running this with the simulator . You should see our "Hello world" message in the simulator.

Running “Hello, world” on real hardware

Next is getting your game running on an actual Playdate console. Connect the Playdate to your computer using the USB cable and make sure the console is awake.

Start your game in the simulator () and then once the simulator starts select > in the menus or use the hotkey .

Uploading the game to the Playdate console takes a few seconds, so be patient. The console will show a message like “Sharing DATA segment with USB. Will reboot when ejected”. You can select the "Home" button in the Playdate console menu to stop the game.

One of my initial hesitations with buying a Playdate was that it didn't originally ship with network connectivity within games, despite supporting Wi-Fi. This is no longer the case, as this year Playdate OS 2.7 added support for HTTP and TCP networking.

So immediately after my "Hello world" game, I wanted to try this new feature. I created the following small application that sends an HTTP request after pressing the button:

First I tried running this program with a local HTTP server on with set to and was able to capture this HTTP request using Wireshark:

0000   47 45 54 20 2f 20 48 54 54 50 2f 31 2e 31 0d 0a   GET / HTTP/1.1..
0010   48 6f 73 74 3a 20 6c 6f 63 61 6c 68 6f 73 74 0d   Host: localhost.
0020   0a 55 73 65 72 2d 41 67 65 6e 74 3a 20 50 6c 61   .User-Agent: Pla
0030   79 64 61 74 65 2f 53 69 6d 0d 0a 43 6f 6e 6e 65   ydate/Sim..Conne
0040   63 74 69 6f 6e 3a 20 63 6c 6f 73 65 0d 0a 0d 0a   ction: close....

So we can see that Playdate HTTP requests are quite minimal, only sending a , and header by default. Keep-Alive and other headers can be optionally configured. The for the Playdate simulator was .

I then tested on real hardware and targeting my own website: with set to . This resulted in the same request being sent, with a of . There's no-doubt lots of experimentation ahead for what's possible with a networked Playdate. That's all for now,

Thanks for keeping RSS alive! ♥

items.title: Why I Built a Pure Python Library for Legacy Office Files (And Why RAG Pipelines Need One)
items.author: Tobias Horsmann
items.content:
If you're building RAG pipelines or document ingestion for LLM agents, you've probably solved the easy part already. Modern Office files? No problem. , , — pick your library, extract your text, move on.

Then someone points your pipeline at an enterprise SharePoint.

Enterprise SharePoints are digital archaeology sites. Marketing uploaded PowerPoints in 2008. Legal has Word documents from 2005. Finance runs on Excel files that predate most of your team's careers.

These aren't edge cases. In my experience, legacy , , and files make up a significant chunk of any long-running enterprise document store. And if you're building a system that needs to ingest "all the documents," you can't just skip them.

Why Existing Solutions Didn't Work for Me

I needed to process these files in AWS Lambda functions for a RAG pipeline. My options were:

The standard answer. Install LibreOffice, run it headless, convert files to text. It works, but it adds over 1GB to your container image. Lambda has a 250MB limit for deployment packages (10GB with container images, but still). Plus, configuring headless LibreOffice is its own adventure.

Solid tool, widely used. But it requires a Java runtime and typically runs as a separate server. That's another service to deploy, monitor, and secure. For a document extraction step in a pipeline, it felt like overkill.

Subprocess calls to command-line tools

Various tools exist that you can shell out to. But subprocess calls are a security concern, they break in restricted environments, and they make your code platform-dependent.

I wanted something simpler: a Python library I could and call.

Building sharepoint-to-text

The core idea: parse both legacy Office binary formats (OLE2) and modern XML-based formats (OOXML) directly in Python. No external dependencies. No subprocess calls. Just text extraction.

  • Legacy formats: , ,
  • Modern formats: , ,

One interface, no conditional logic, no format detection boilerplate in your code.

Why This Matters for RAG and LLM Agents

If you're building document ingestion for RAG, you're probably dealing with heterogeneous input. Users upload files. Pipelines crawl document stores. You can't control what formats show up.

The typical approach is a cascade of if-statements and multiple libraries:

With sharepoint-to-text, it's just:

The library figures out the format and handles it appropriately.

Because it's pure Python with no system dependencies:

  • Container images stay small — no LibreOffice bloat
  • — works in Lambda, Cloud Functions, Azure Functions
  • — no subprocess calls, no shell execution
  • — Windows, macOS, Linux, whatever
  • You're building RAG pipelines against enterprise document stores
  • Your LLM agent needs to process user-uploaded files of unknown vintage
  • You're deploying to serverless with size constraints
  • Your security team doesn't allow subprocess execution
  • You're tired of maintaining LibreOffice in containers
pip sharepoint-to-text

I'd appreciate feedback, especially if you hit edge cases with specific file types. Legacy Office formats are notoriously inconsistent, and real-world files are the best test suite.
